beats.title: Invocation and Setting for the Linga Purana
beats.summary: The chapter opens with a salutation to Rudra, Hari, Brahma, and the Supreme Self. The sage Narada, after worshipping Shiva at various sacred sites, arrives at Naimisha forest. The assembled sages welcome him and request Suta Romaharshana, a disciple of Vyasa, to recite the Purana. Suta, after paying homage to Mahadeva, Brahma, Janardana, and Vyasa, begins to describe the Linga, the form of the Supreme Brahman, encompassing the Vedas and the three gunas.

beats.title: Introduction to the Linga Purana
beats.summary: This chapter from the Linga Purana introduces the scope of the entire Purana, listing the topics to be covered: creation, the origin of the Linga, the story of Brahma and Vishnu's dispute, the birth of Rudra, the lineage of sages, the description of various kalpas, the glory of Varanasi, the story of Daksha's sacrifice, the marriage of Shiva, and many other mythological and philosophical themes. It also mentions that the Purana was originally vast but was condensed by Vyasa into 11,000 verses.

beats.title: Table of Contents of the Liṅga Purāṇa
beats.summary: This chapter from the Linga Purana (Part 1, Chapter 2) is a table of contents listing the topics to be covered in the Purana. It enumerates various narratives, teachings, and genealogies, including the birth of Nandi, the story of Pativrata, the conflict between Vṛtra and Indra, the avatāras of Parāśara, Vyāsa, and Śuka, the deeds of Viṣṇu and Rudra, and many other subjects related to dharma, rituals, and cosmology.

beats.title: The Nature of the Linga and Creation
beats.summary: This chapter from the Linga Purana discusses the nature of the Linga (symbol of Shiva) and the unmanifest (aliṅga) as the supreme reality. It describes the creation of the universe from the unmanifest through the interaction of Shiva and Prakriti, and lists various events and incarnations related to Shiva, Vishnu, and other deities. The text emphasizes the supremacy of the Linga form and the path to liberation through understanding this knowledge.

beats.title: Cosmic Creation from the Unmanifest
beats.summary: This chapter from the Linga Purana describes the process of cosmic creation (sṛṣṭi) from the unmanifest (avyakta) through the evolution of mahat, ahaṃkāra, and the five tanmātras (sound, touch, form, taste, smell) into the five gross elements (ākāśa, vāyu, agni, ap, pṛthivī). It then explains the formation of the cosmic egg (aṇḍa) with its seven coverings, and the emergence of Brahmā, Viṣṇu, and Rudra as aspects of Maheśvara (Śiva) who governs creation, preservation, and dissolution. The chapter also introduces the concept of prākṛta sarga (primary creation) and the cycle of day and night for Brahmā.

beats.title: Cosmic Time Cycles
beats.summary: This chapter from the Linga Purana describes the cosmic time cycles, including the divisions of a day of Brahma, the four yugas, and various units of time from the human to the divine scale. It explains how the day and night of Brahma correspond to creation and dissolution, and details the lengths of the yugas and their sandhyas.

beats.title: Cosmic Time Cycles
beats.summary: This chapter from the Linga Purana describes the cosmic time cycles: yugas, manvantaras, kalpas, and the lifespan of Brahma. It provides numerical details of the durations of Krita, Treta, Dvapara, and Kali yugas, the composition of a manvantara, and the number of kalpas in Brahma's life. It also lists various names of kalpas and mentions the beings that reside in higher worlds during pralaya.

beats.title: Cosmic Cycles and Creation
beats.summary: This chapter from the Linga Purana describes the cosmic cycles of creation and dissolution under the control of Maheshvara (Shiva). It details the role of Brahma in creation, the emergence of various sargas (creations) including the mukhya sarga, and the birth of the mind-born sons of Brahma such as Sanaka, Sananda, Sanatana, and others. The chapter emphasizes the supremacy of Shiva as the ultimate cause and the cyclical nature of time.

beats.title: Creation of the Sages and the Lineage of Daksha
beats.summary: This chapter from the Linga Purana describes the creation of various beings by Brahma, including sages, deities, and the lineage of Daksha and Prasuti. It details the marriages of Daksha's daughters to various sages and gods, and the origin of Sati as the daughter of Daksha and her marriage to Rudra (Shiva). The chapter also introduces the concept of Ardhanarishvara and the origin of the eleven Rudras.

beats.title: Genealogies of the Prajapatis and the Birth of Agni and the Pitrs
beats.summary: This chapter from the Linga Purana describes the progeny of Dharma and other sages, including the births of various deities, sages, and cosmic beings. It details the lineages of the Prajapatis, the births of Agni (fire) in three forms, and the origins of the Pitrs (ancestors). The chapter emphasizes the interconnectedness of creation through marriages and offspring.

beats.title: Creation of Rudras and the Boon of Mortal Beings
beats.summary: This chapter from the Linga Purana describes the creation of Rudras from Shiva's mind, the request by Brahma to create mortal beings, and the teaching that those who take refuge in Shiva are liberated from hell. It also discusses the qualities of detachment and the nature of the supreme being.

beats.title: The Secret of Shiva's Grace and the Lineage of Vyasas
beats.summary: This chapter from the Linga Purana discusses the secret of Shiva's grace and the yoga path. It lists the Vyasas and Rudras across different manvantaras and kalpas, emphasizing the lineage of yoga teachers and the importance of grace for liberation.

beats.title: Chapter 0 (part 13)
beats.summary: This chapter from the Linga Purana lists the names of sages and yogis who appear as incarnations of the Supreme Lord in different ages and manvantaras, particularly in the Vaivasvata Manvantara. It enumerates the seven Manus, the yogavataras, and the disciples of the yogeshvaras, emphasizing the continuity of spiritual teaching through lineages.

beats.title: The Pashupata Yoga and Its Disciples
beats.summary: This chapter from the Linga Purana lists sages who are disciples of the Pashupata yoga tradition, explains the meaning of 'Pashupati', and describes the eight limbs of yoga (ashtanga yoga) including yama, niyama, asana, pranayama, pratyahara, dharana, dhyana, and samadhi, with detailed exposition on yama and niyama.

beats.title: Duties of a Householder and the Path of Purity
beats.summary: This chapter from the Linga Purana (Part 1, Chapter 8, verses 17-41) discusses the duties and practices of a householder (gṛhastha), emphasizing celibacy (brahmacarya) within marriage, non-violence (ahiṃsā), detachment from sensual pleasures, and the importance of internal purity over external rituals. It outlines the ten niyamas (observances) including cleanliness, austerity, charity, self-study, and devotion to Śiva. The text stresses that true purity comes from inner knowledge and detachment, not merely from bathing in holy waters.

beats.title: Yogic Practices and Pranayama
beats.summary: This chapter from the Linga Purana (Part 1, Chapter 8, verses 41-65) expounds on the yogic practices of pratyahara (withdrawal of senses), dharana (concentration), dhyana (meditation), and samadhi (absorption), with a detailed focus on pranayama (breath control). It describes the types of pranayama (low, medium, high), their effects, and the resulting states of shanti, prashanti, dipti, and prasada. It also enumerates the ten vital airs (prana, apana, samana, udana, vyana, naga, kurma, krikala, devadatta, dhananjaya) and their functions.

beats.title: Yoga and the Ten Vayus
beats.summary: This chapter from the Linga Purana (Part 1, Chapter 8, verses 65-89) details the names and functions of the ten vital airs (vayus) in the body, the names of the intellect (buddhi) and its modifications, and provides extensive instructions on the practice of yoga, including suitable locations, postures, and the process of pranayama and meditation to purify the mind and attain spiritual realization.

beats.title: Instructions on Shiva Dhyana
beats.summary: This chapter from the Linga Purana provides detailed instructions for meditation on Shiva, focusing on the placement of awareness at various bodily centers (navel, heart, throat, between eyebrows, forehead, crown) and the visualization of light forms. It describes the process of transcending the three gunas (tamas, rajas, sattva) and attaining a state of pure consciousness identified with Shiva, culminating in samadhi. The text emphasizes the use of breath control (kumbhaka) and the merging of the individual self with the supreme reality.

beats.title: Obstacles and Siddhis in Yoga
beats.summary: This chapter from the Linga Purana discusses the obstacles (antarāyas) in yoga practice, including laziness, disease, doubt, lack of faith, and distractions. It describes the six minor siddhis (pratibhā, śravaṇā, vārttā, darśanā, āsvādā, vedanā) that arise when obstacles are overcome, and emphasizes the need for perseverance and guru's grace.

beats.title: The Hierarchy of Yogic Powers
beats.summary: This chapter from the Linga Purana describes the progressive stages of yogic attainment (aiśvarya) corresponding to the elements (earth, water, fire, air, ether) and higher realms (Indra, Soma, Prajāpati, Brahma). It details the specific powers (guṇas) associated with each element and the need to transcend them to reach the supreme bliss of Brahman.

beats.title: The Vision of the Yogi and the Path to Liberation
beats.summary: This chapter from the Linga Purana describes the nature of the world (saṃsāra), the qualities of the mind, and the path to liberation through detachment and yoga. It emphasizes the importance of renouncing siddhis (supernatural powers) as obstacles, and the realization of Śiva within through the lamp of self-knowledge. The text also lists various beings and powers seen by the accomplished yogi.

beats.title: Characteristics of a Sādhu and Dharma
beats.summary: This chapter from the Linga Purana discusses the characteristics of a 'sādhu' (virtuous person) and the definitions of dharma and adharma. It describes the four āśramas (stages of life) and the qualities of an ācārya (teacher). The text emphasizes self-control, non-attachment, truthfulness, and the practice of yoga as means to attain liberation.

beats.title: The Dialogue of Devotion: Shiva and Parvati in Varanasi
beats.summary: This chapter from the Linga Purana discusses the path of devotion (bhakti) as the supreme means to attain liberation. It emphasizes that detachment, renunciation, and knowledge are important, but devotion to Lord Shiva (Śaṅkara) is the ultimate way. The chapter recounts a dialogue between Shiva and Parvati in Varanasi, where Shiva explains how he is accessible through devotion, and narrates how Brahma once saw him in five forms (Sadyojāta, Vāma, Tatpuruṣa, Aghora, Īśāna) corresponding to colors. The teaching highlights that even the unworthy can be saved by devotion, and that bhakti surpasses all other practices.

beats.title: The Emergence of Shiva's Five Faces and the Power of Shraddha
beats.summary: This chapter from the Linga Purana describes the emergence of the five faces of Shiva (Sadyojata, Vamadeva, Aghora, Tatpurusha, Ishana) through Brahma's meditation in different kalpas (cosmic ages). It emphasizes the power of shraddha (faith) and dhyana (meditation) to realize the supreme lord. The chapter also narrates the birth of sages and disciples associated with each face.

beats.title: The Origin of the Raudri Gayatri and the Yoga of Liberation
beats.summary: This chapter describes the emergence of the Gayatri mantra as a form of the goddess Rudrani, bestowed by Mahadeva upon Brahma during the Pita-vasa kalpa. It details Brahma's meditation, vision of the four-footed goddess, and the subsequent teaching of yoga to sages who then merge into Rudra.

beats.title: The Kṛṣṇavarṇa Kalpa and the Aghora Mantra
beats.summary: This chapter from the Linga Purana describes the transition between kalpas, focusing on the Kṛṣṇavarṇa kalpa. Brahmā, desiring to create, meditates and produces a dark-colored son, Aghora, who manifests along with four other dark sages. They practice yoga and attain liberation. The chapter then details the Aghora mantra and its efficacy in absolving various sins, including brahmahatyā, with specific numbers of repetitions.

beats.title: Expiatory Rites and the Viśvarūpa Kalpa
beats.summary: This chapter from the Linga Purana describes the expiatory rites for various sins, including the chanting of the Aghora mantra, the use of cow products, and the performance of homa. It also introduces the next kalpa of Brahma, where the goddess Sarasvati appears as Viśvarūpā.

beats.title: Brahma's Praise of Shiva and the Revelation of Viśvarūpā
beats.summary: Brahma praises the supreme deity Shiva, describing his universal form and attributes. He asks about the nature of the goddess Viśvarūpā, and Shiva explains the cosmic cycle and the significance of the current kalpa.

beats.title: The Nature of the Linga and the Supreme Being
beats.summary: This chapter from the Linga Purana describes the nature of the supreme being, the origin of Prakriti (the primordial nature) with thirty-two attributes, the creation of Kumārakas from the goddess, and the story of the Linga. It also recounts the cosmic dissolution and the emergence of the supreme being as Nārāyaṇa, Viṣṇu, and Śaṅkara.

beats.title: The Dispute of Brahma and Vishnu and the Appearance of the Linga
beats.summary: Brahma and Vishnu argue over who is supreme, then witness a cosmic linga of fire. They decide to find its ends; Vishnu goes downward, Brahma upward. The linga symbolizes the supreme reality beyond both.

beats.title: The Search for the Liṅga's Origin
beats.summary: This chapter from the Linga Purana describes the search for the origin of the cosmic Liṅga by Brahmā and Viṣṇu. Brahmā takes the form of a swan (haṃsa) and flies upward, while Viṣṇu takes the form of a boar (varāha) and digs downward, but neither can find the end. They then witness the manifestation of the sacred syllable Oṃ from the Liṅga, which reveals the three varṇas (A, U, M) and the transcendent state beyond. Viṣṇu, through the ṛṣi (seer) within, realizes the supreme Brahman.

beats.title: The Cosmic Syllable Om and the Body of Maheshvara
beats.summary: This chapter from the Linga Purana describes the cosmic significance of the syllable Om, equating its three components (A, U, M) with the divine functions of creation, preservation, and dissolution. It details how the primordial sound manifests as the cosmic egg, from which Brahma is born. The text then maps the Sanskrit alphabet onto the body of Maheshvara (Shiva), and concludes with Vishnu's vision of the supreme mantra.

beats.title: Hymn to Shiva from the Linga Purana
beats.summary: This chapter from the Linga Purana contains a hymn of praise to Lord Shiva, describing his various forms, attributes, and cosmic functions. The hymn is recited by Brahma or a devotee, invoking Shiva as the supreme deity associated with the five elements, the Vedas, and the mantra 'Om'. It emphasizes Shiva's role in creation, preservation, and destruction, and his manifestation as the linga.

beats.title: Shiva Sahasranama Stotra (Part)
beats.summary: This chapter is a hymn of praise to Lord Shiva, recited by Vishnu, consisting of 42 verses. It glorifies Shiva through various names and attributes, describing his cosmic and transcendent nature. The hymn is said to purify sins and lead to the world of Brahma.

beats.title: The Revelation of the Linga and the Cosmic Sleep of Vishnu
beats.summary: Mahadeva reveals to Brahma and Vishnu that he is the source of both, manifesting as the threefold Brahma-Vishnu-Shiva for creation, preservation, and dissolution. He explains that the linga is his symbol, and the Linga Purana's recitation leads to liberation. The chapter also describes Vishnu's cosmic sleep on the serpent Shesha in the primeval ocean.

beats.title: The Mutual Exploration of Brahma and Vishnu
beats.summary: In this chapter from the Linga Purana, the cosmic play of the Supreme Being is depicted. Brahma emerges from a lotus on Vishnu's navel and encounters Vishnu lying on the waters. A dialogue ensues where each claims primacy. Vishnu enters Brahma's mouth, explores the universe within, and then Brahma enters Vishnu's belly, also failing to find an end. The chapter highlights the mutual incomprehensibility of the divine forms and the illusory nature of their separation.

beats.title: The Cosmic Conflict and Reconciliation of Brahma and Vishnu
beats.summary: Brahma and Vishnu engage in a cosmic conflict, with Brahma entering Vishnu's body and later emerging from his navel via a lotus stalk. Shiva appears as a third force, and the two gods reconcile, with Vishnu asking Brahma to become his son.

beats.title: The Debate of Supremacy and the Revelation of Shiva as the Supreme
beats.summary: Brahma and Vishnu debate the supremacy of Shiva, who appears as a fiery pillar (linga). Vishnu explains that Shiva is the supreme being, the seed of all, while Brahma is the womb. The chapter establishes the doctrine of Shiva as the ultimate reality, both manifest and unmanifest.

beats.title: Cosmic Creation and the Hymn to Shiva
beats.summary: This chapter from the Linga Purana describes the cosmic egg (Hiranyagarbha) emerging from the primordial waters, its division into heaven and earth, and the birth of Brahma and the mind-born sages (Sanaka, Sanandana, Sanatana, Sanatkumara, and Rbhu). It also includes a hymn of praise to Shiva by Vishnu, highlighting the supremacy of Shiva.

beats.title: Hymn of Praise to the Supreme Source
beats.summary: This chapter is a hymn of praise to the supreme being, acknowledging him as the source and lord of all creation, including the Vedas, smritis, karma, yoga, sankhya, celestial bodies, elements, geography, time units, puranas, manvantaras, beings, and various classes of entities. It extols his manifold forms and attributes.

beats.title: Hymn to Rudra
beats.summary: This chapter is a hymn of praise to Rudra/Shiva, listing numerous names and attributes, and offering obeisance to various forms and aspects of the deity. It is part of the Linga Purana, focusing on the worship of Shiva as the supreme being.

beats.title: Hymn to Shiva's Cosmic Form
beats.summary: This chapter is a hymn of praise to Lord Shiva, describing his various forms, attributes, and cosmic functions. It includes a series of epithets and invocations, highlighting his role as destroyer of demons and poison, his fierce appearance, and his connection to the universe. The text also describes the cosmic body of Shiva, equating different parts of the universe with his body parts.

beats.title: Hymn of Praise to Shiva and the Boon
beats.summary: This chapter from the Linga Purana contains a hymn of praise to Shiva (Rudra) recited by Brahma and Vishnu, followed by Shiva's response. The hymn extols Shiva's various attributes, forms, and cosmic roles. After the hymn, Shiva appears pleased and addresses Brahma and Vishnu, acknowledging their devotion and granting them a boon. Vishnu asks for unwavering devotion to Shiva.

beats.title: Shiva's Grace and Brahma's Creation of Rudras
beats.summary: Shiva grants Brahma devotion and then departs. Brahma, attempting to create, becomes frustrated and sheds tears that become serpents. In anger, he faints and dies, but from his body arise eleven Rudras. Shiva revives Brahma, who then sees the universe in Shiva and praises him. Shiva explains the Śvetakalpa and the Sadyojāta mantra.

beats.title: Rudra's Manifestations in Different Yugas
beats.summary: Rudra explains to the sage his manifestations in different yugas as various forms (Lohita, Vamadeva, Tatpurusha, Ghora, Vishvarupa) corresponding to the colors of the Gayatri, and how those who know these forms attain liberation.

beats.title: Rudra's Discourse on Gayatri and the Cosmic Order
beats.summary: Rudra explains to Brahma the cosmic significance of the Gayatri mantra, describing how the four-footed and two-footed beings arise from its forms, and how the seven worlds correspond to its feet. He reveals that those who meditate on him through Gayatri attain liberation.

beats.title: Shiva's Prophecy of Incarnations
beats.summary: Shiva (Mahadeva) reveals to Brahma that he cannot be seen by humans through tapas, rituals, or study, but only through meditation (dhyana). He then prophesies his future incarnations in each Dvapara Yuga of the Vaivasvata Manvantara, along with his disciples, who will attain Rudraloka through yoga.

beats.title: The Incarnations of Vyasa and the Speaker in Each Dvapara
beats.summary: This chapter from the Linga Purana describes the future incarnations of Vyasa in each Dvapara Yuga and the corresponding incarnations of the speaker (a sage) as a disciple or teacher, along with their disciples. It lists the names of Vyasa, the speaker's alias, and the disciples who attain Rudraloka through yoga.

beats.title: The Successive Incarnations of Vyasa and the Speaker
beats.summary: This chapter from the Linga Purana describes the future incarnations of the sage Vyasa in each of the fourteen (and beyond) Dvapara yugas, along with the speaker's own incarnations as a disciple of Vyasa and the names of his sons who attain Rudraloka through Maheshvara yoga.

beats.title: The Successive Incarnations of Vyasa and the Devotee of Shiva
beats.summary: This chapter from the Linga Purana describes the future incarnations of the sage Vyasa and the speaker (a devotee of Shiva) across successive cosmic cycles (parivartas). It lists the names of Vyasa in each cycle, the corresponding name and location of the speaker, and the disciples who attain Rudraloka through the practice of Maheshvara Yoga, emphasizing the importance of meditation on Shiva and the holy site Mahalaya.

beats.title: The Future Incarnations of Vyasa and His Disciples
beats.summary: This chapter from the Linga Purana describes the future incarnations of Vyasa across various parivartas (cycles) and the corresponding births of the speaker (a sage) as different disciples or sons of Vyasa, all devoted to Rudra. It lists the names of Vyasa, the speaker, and their disciples, emphasizing the attainment of Rudraloka through Maheshvara yoga.

beats.title: Shiva's Avatars and the Pashupata Yoga
beats.summary: In this chapter from the Linga Purana, Lord Shiva describes his future incarnations and the Pashupata yoga path. He reveals that in the 28th cycle, he will incarnate as Lakulisha, with disciples Kushika, Garga, Mitra, and Kaurushya. The chapter emphasizes the supremacy of the five-syllable mantra (Om Namah Shivaya) and the Pashupata vow for liberation, stating that without it, even Sankhya and Pancharatra paths fail. Brahma questions why Vishnu, who is supreme, worships Shiva's linga; Shiva explains that all deities, including Vishnu and Brahma, worship the linga to attain their positions.

beats.title: The Origin and Ritual of Linga Worship
beats.summary: This chapter from the Linga Purana describes the origin and importance of the linga worship, focusing on the bath (snāna) ritual before worshiping Śiva. It recounts how Śiva taught the linga worship to Brahmā, who then passed it on to Nandī, Sanatkumāra, and Vyāsa. The chapter details the three types of baths (vāruṇa, āgneya, mantra) and emphasizes inner purity over external cleansing.

beats.title: Ritual Bathing and Worship
beats.summary: This chapter from the Linga Purana (Part 1, Chapter 25-26) provides detailed instructions on the ritual of bathing (snāna) and the subsequent worship of Gāyatrī and the sun, including the five great sacrifices (pañca-mahāyajña). It emphasizes purification, mantra recitation, and proper offerings to deities, sages, and ancestors.

beats.title: The Five Great Sacrifices and the Procedure of Brahma-yajña
beats.summary: This chapter from the Linga Purana describes the five great sacrifices (pañca mahāyajña), with emphasis on the Brahma-yajña (sacrifice to Brahman). It details the procedure for performing Brahma-yajña, including purification rituals, recitation of Vedas, and application of sacred ash (bhasma). The chapter also explains the consequences of neglecting these sacrifices and the importance of performing them for spiritual upliftment.

beats.title: Ritual Worship of Shiva
beats.summary: This chapter from the Linga Purana describes the detailed procedure for the ritual worship of Shiva, including purification, preparation of offerings, and meditation on the deity's form. It emphasizes the use of mantras, especially the Pranava and the five-syllable mantra, and outlines the steps for offering arghya, pādya, and ācamana. The chapter also mentions the worship of attendant deities like Nandin, Skanda, Vināyaka, and the goddess.

beats.title: Ritual Bathing of the Liṅga
beats.summary: This chapter from the Linga Purana describes the ritual of bathing (snāpana) the Śiva liṅga with various mantras and substances, detailing the preparation of the seat, invocation of deities, and the specific mantras to be used for purification and liberation from sins.

beats.title: Linga Worship and Internal Meditation
beats.summary: This chapter from the Linga Purana describes the detailed procedure for worshipping the Shiva Linga, including offerings of sandalwood paste, flowers, incense, lamps, food, water, and other items. It then transitions to internal worship (ābhyantara pūjā), explaining the meditation on the form of Shiva as both niṣkala (formless) and sakala (with form), the concept of Purusha, the 25 tattvas, and the supremacy of Shiva as the 26th principle beyond Prakriti and Purusha. The chapter also discusses the relationship between the macrocosm and microcosm, and the role of time and karma.
